captions.text: every green leaf is a tiny solar-powered factory. it turns light, air, and water into food.
sunlight pours in from above, and carbon dioxide drifts in from the air through tiny pores in the leaf.
water climbs up from the roots. light, carbon dioxide, and water — those are the three raw ingredients.
and the leaf hands back oxygen, the air we breathe. now let's go inside and watch it happen.
slice a leaf open and you find layers of cells stacked like bricks, all packed with green.
let's zoom into one of these mesophyll cells — the leaf's main food-making workers.
it has a tough wall, a thin membrane, a nucleus, and a big watery vacuole in the middle.
and scattered around the edge: dozens of little green ovals called chloroplasts. that's where the magic happens.
here's a single chloroplast, blown up huge. it has a double outer membrane like a sealed pouch.
inside floats a thick fluid called the stroma — that's where sugar gets built.
suspended in it are stacks of green discs. each disc is a thylakoid; a stack of them is a granum.
thin tubes called lamellae connect the stacks. the green discs are packed with chlorophyll, the pigment that catches light.
two homes for two jobs: the discs catch light, the fluid builds sugar. let's watch each one.
first, the light reactions, right on the thylakoid membrane. these green machines are embedded in it.
sunlight strikes photosystem two and energizes electrons. to replace them, it splits water apart.
splitting water releases oxygen — the gas we breathe out — plus hydrogen ions and those electrons.
the excited electrons hop down a chain of carriers, and their energy pumps hydrogen ions into the inner space.
photosystem one re-energizes them with more light to build NADPH, an energy carrier.
and the crowded hydrogen ions rush back out through ATP synthase, spinning it to make ATP. two fuels, ready.
now the second half — the calvin cycle, running in the stroma. it doesn't need light directly.
it spends the ATP and NADPH the light reactions just made.
step one: an enzyme called rubisco grabs carbon dioxide from the air and sticks it onto a sugar, RuBP.
step two: using ATP and NADPH, that's converted into G3P — the first real sugar.
step three: most G3P is recycled to rebuild RuBP, so the cycle can turn again.
the rest leaves as G3P and joins up into glucose. that's the food the whole plant runs on.
so here's the whole thing in one line.
six carbon dioxide, plus six water, plus the energy of light...
...become one glucose molecule, and six oxygen released to the air.
light and simple raw materials in; food and breathable air out. that's photosynthesis.
so that's the whole journey: sunlight to leaf, to cell, to chloroplast, through two reaction stages, into sugar.
photosynthesis — how the whole living world runs on light.
